perturbation.original_text: Regardless of the form, format, or media on or in which the Confidential Information is provided and regardless of whether any such Confidential Information is marked as such, “Confidential Information” means (1) any and all information provided by or made available by the State to the Contractor in connection with the Contract and (2) any and all personally identifiable information (PII) (including but not limited to personal information as defined in Md. Ann. Code, General Provisions §4- 101(h) and Md. Ann. Code, State Govt. § 10- 1301(c)) and protected health information (PHI) as defined in 45 CFR 160.103 that is provided by a person or entity to the Contractor in connection with this Contrac t. Confidential Information includes, by way of example only, information that the Contractor views, takes notes from, copies (if the State agrees in writing to permit copying), possesses or is otherwise provided access to and use of by the State in relati on to the Contract.
perturbation.changed_text: Regardless of the form, format, or media on or in which the information is provided and regardless of whether any such information is marked as such, “Confidential Information” means any and all information provided by or made available by the State to the Contractor in connection with the Contract. Confidential Information includes, by way of example only, information that the Contractor views, takes notes from, copies (if the State agrees in writing to permit copying), possesses or is otherwise provided access to and use of by the State in relation to the Contract.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit inclusion of PII and PHI within the definition of 'Confidential Information', the contract creates an in-text contradiction. Later sections, specifically Section 8 regarding irreparable harm and indemnification, assume that breaches related to PII and PHI are covered. The removal creates ambiguity and thus a contradiction because it is unclear if these types of data are adequately protected under the agreement terms, creating uncertainty in enforcement regarding PII and PHI breaches.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Contractor acknowledges that any failur e by the Contractor or the Contractor’s Personnel to abide by the terms and conditions of use of the Confidential Information may cause irreparable harm to the State and that monetary damages may be inadequate to compensate the State for such breach. Accor dingly, the Contractor agrees that the State may obtain an injunction to prevent the disclosure, copying or improper use of the Confidential Information. The Contractor consents to personal jurisdiction in the Maryland State Courts. The State’s rights and remedies hereunder are cumulative and the State expressly reserves any and all rights, remedies, claims and actions that it may have now or in the future to protect the Confidential Information and seek damages from the Contractor and the Contractor’s Personnel for a failure to comply with the requirements of this Agreement. In the event the State suffers any losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, or costs (including, by way of example only, attorneys’ fees and disbursements) that are attributable, in whole or in part to any failure by the Contractor or any of the Contractor’s Personnel to comply with the requirements of this Agreement, the Contractor shall hold harmless and indemnify the State from and against any such losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, and costs.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly included PII and PHI in the definition of 'Confidential Information'. The changed text removes this explicit inclusion. The contradicted text outlines remedies for breaches related to 'Confidential Information'. Because PII and PHI are no longer explicitly defined as Confidential Information, it creates a contradiction because it is unclear whether the remedies outlined in the contradicted text apply to breaches of PII and PHI. This creates uncertainty and potential unenforceability, fitting the Omissions - In Text Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: The Contractor shall limit access to the Confidential Information to the Contractor’s Personnel who have a demonstrable need to know such Confidential Information in order to perform under the Contract an d who are bound by confidentiality obligations at least as restr ictive as those set forth in this Agreement. Contractor shall provide copies of such agreements to the State. The names of such personnel are attached hereto and made a part hereof as Attach ment I-2. Contractor shall update Attachment I-2 by adding additional names (whether Contractor’s Personnel or a subcontractor’s personnel) as needed, from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor shall limit access to the Confidential Information to the Contractor’s Personnel who have a demonstrable need to know such Confidential Information in order to perform under the Contract. The names of such personnel are attached hereto and made a part hereof as Attach ment I-2. Contractor shall update Attachment I-2 by adding additional names (whether Contractor’s Personnel or a subcontractor’s personnel) as needed, from time to time.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement that Contractor's Personnel be 'bound by confidentiality obligations at least as restrictive as those set forth in this Agreement', a contradiction is created. Section 7 states that 'A breach of this Agreement by the Contractor or the Contractor’s Personnel shall constitute a breach of the Contract', but if personnel are not explicitly bound by confidentiality agreements as strict as this one, it's unclear if their actions would trigger Section 7, thus creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: A breach of this Agreement by the Contractor or the Contractor’s Personnel shall constitute a breach of the Contract between the Contractor and the State.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that Contractor's Personnel accessing Confidential Information are 'bound by confidentiality obligations at least as restrictive as those set forth in this Agreement.' The changed text removes this requirement. The contradicted text states that 'A breach of this Agreement by the Contractor or the Contractor’s Personnel shall constitute a breach of the Contract'. By removing the confidentiality requirement for personnel, it becomes unclear whether their actions constitute a breach of the agreement, as they are no longer explicitly bound by the same confidentiality standards. This creates a direct conflict, as the condition for triggering a breach becomes ambiguous, thus creating an in-text contradiction as personnel might not be bound by the same terms.

perturbation.original_text: If the Contractor intends to disseminate any portion of the Confidential Information to non-employee agents who are assisting in the Contractor’s performance of the Contract or will otherwise Attac hment I – Non-Disclosure Agreement (Contractor) Page I-2 effective date: April 17, 2018 have a role in performing any aspect of the Contract, the Contra ctor shall first obtain the written consent of the State to any such dissemination. The State may grant, deny, or condition any such consent, as it may deem appropriate in its sole and absolute subjective discretion.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Contractor intends to disseminate any portion of the Confidential Information to non-employee agents who are assisting in the Contractor’s performance of the Contract or will otherwise Attac hment I – Non-Disclosure Agreement (Contractor) Page I-2 effective date: April 17, 2018 have a role in performing any aspect of the Contract, the Contractor shall notify the State of any such dissemination.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall first obtain the written consent of the State' to 'shall notify the State', the contract now contradicts Section 8 regarding the State's ability to seek an injunction. Section 8 presumes the State has control over dissemination, but simple notification removes this control, creating an in-text contradiction on whether the State can prevent dissemination.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Contractor acknowledges that any failur e by the Contractor or the Contractor’s Personnel to abide by the terms and conditions of use of the Confidential Information may cause irreparable harm to the State and that monetary damages may be inadequate to compensate the State for such breach. Accor dingly, the Contractor agrees that the State may obtain an injunction to prevent the disclosure, copying or improper use of the Confidential Information. The Contractor consents to personal jurisdiction in the Maryland State Courts. The State’s rights and remedies hereunder are cumulative and the State expressly reserves any and all rights, remedies, claims and actions that it may have now or in the future to protect the Confidential Information and seek damages from the Contractor and the Contractor’s Personnel for a failure to comply with the requirements of this Agreement. In the event the State suffers any losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, or costs (including, by way of example only, attorneys’ fees and disbursements) that are attributable, in whole or in part to any failure by the Contractor or any of the Contractor’s Personnel to comply with the requirements of this Agreement, the Contractor shall hold harmless and indemnify the State from and against any such losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, and costs.
perturbation.justification: The original text required the Contractor to obtain written consent from the State before disseminating Confidential Information. The changed text modifies this to simply require the Contractor to notify the State of any such dissemination. This change directly contradicts the contradicted text, which grants the State the right to seek an injunction to prevent the disclosure, copying, or improper use of Confidential Information. By removing the State's control over dissemination (consent requirement), the State's ability to obtain an injunction is undermined, because they are no longer in a position to 'prevent the disclosure' in the same way. Thus, the change creates a clear in-text contradiction regarding the State's power to control Confidential Information, meeting the definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Contr actor shall promptly advise the State in writing if it learns of any unauthorized use, misappropriation, or disclosure of the Confidential Information by any of the Contractor’s Personnel or the Contractor’s former Personnel. Contractor shall, at its own e xpense, cooperate with the State in seeking injunctive or other equitable relief against any such person(s).
perturbation.changed_text: The Contr actor shall promptly advise the State in writing if it learns of any unauthorized use, misappropriation, or disclosure of the Confidential Information by any of the Contractor’s Personnel or the Contractor’s former Personnel.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'Contractor shall, at its own e xpense, cooperate with the State in seeking injunctive or other equitable relief against any such person(s)' directly contradicts the State's rights and remedies outlined in Section 8. Section 8 implies the Contractor will actively help protect Confidential Information, but this removal means the Contractor only needs to notify, not assist. This creates an in-text contradiction about the level of support the Contractor is obligated to provide, and the expectation of active support.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Contractor acknowledges that any failur e by the Contractor or the Contractor’s Personnel to abide by the terms and conditions of use of the Confidential Information may cause irreparable harm to the State and that monetary damages may be inadequate to compensate the State for such breach. Accor dingly, the Contractor agrees that the State may obtain an injunction to prevent the disclosure, copying or improper use of the Confidential Information. The Contractor consents to personal jurisdiction in the Maryland State Courts. The State’s rights and remedies hereunder are cumulative and the State expressly reserves any and all rights, remedies, claims and actions that it may have now or in the future to protect the Confidential Information and seek damages from the Contractor and the Contractor’s Personnel for a failure to comply with the requirements of this Agreement. In the event the State suffers any losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, or costs (including, by way of example only, attorneys’ fees and disbursements) that are attributable, in whole or in part to any failure by the Contractor or any of the Contractor’s Personnel to comply with the requirements of this Agreement, the Contractor shall hold harmless and indemnify the State from and against any such losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, and costs.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the 'Contractor shall, at its own expense, cooperate with the State in seeking injunctive or other equitable relief'. This obligates the contractor to actively assist. The changed text removes this obligation, only requiring the contractor to notify the State. The contradicted text states 'the State’s rights and remedies hereunder are cumulative and the State expressly reserves any and all rights, remedies, claims and actions that it may have now or in the future to protect the Confidential Information'. This implies the State expects the Contractor to cooperate in protecting confidential information, as initially stated in the original text. The change creates a contradiction because the Contractor is no longer explicitly obligated to assist, conflicting with the State's expectation and reserved rights as outlined in the contradicted text, as well as creating a situation where the 'State's rights and remedies' are reduced due to the diminished support, fitting the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: The Contractor shall, at its own expense, return to the State all Confidential Information in its care, custody, control or possession upon request of the State or on termination of the Contract.
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor shall return to the State all Confidential Information in its care, custody, control or possession upon request of the State or on termination of the Contract.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'at its own expense', the contract now implies that the State might bear the cost of returning the confidential information. This creates a contradiction with Section 8, which states the Contractor shall indemnify the State for any losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, or costs. If the State has to pay for the return, is that 'indemnifiable' under Section 8, or does the omission mean the State assumes that cost? This creates ambiguity, resulting in a contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Contractor acknowledges that any failur e by the Contractor or the Contractor’s Personnel to abide by the terms and conditions of use of the Confidential Information may cause irreparable harm to the State and that monetary damages may be inadequate to compensate the State for such breach. Accor dingly, the Contractor agrees that the State may obtain an injunction to prevent the disclosure, copying or improper use of the Confidential Information. The Contractor consents to personal jurisdiction in the Maryland State Courts. The State’s rights and remedies hereunder are cumulative and the State expressly reserves any and all rights, remedies, claims and actions that it may have now or in the future to protect the Confidential Information and seek damages from the Contractor and the Contractor’s Personnel for a failure to comply with the requirements of this Agreement. In the event the State suffers any losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, or costs (including, by way of example only, attorneys’ fees and disbursements) that are attributable, in whole or in part to any failure by the Contractor or any of the Contractor’s Personnel to comply with the requirements of this Agreement, the Contractor shall hold harmless and indemnify the State from and against any such losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, and costs.
perturbation.justification: The original text states 'The Contractor shall, at its own expense...'. The changed text removes 'at its own expense', which implies that the cost of returning confidential information might be borne by the State. This directly contradicts the provided 'Contradicted Text', which states 'the Contractor shall hold harmless and indemnify the State from and against any such losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, and costs'. If the State pays for the return, this creates a direct conflict with the indemnification clause, creating a strong in-text contradiction based on the definition of 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: A breach of this Agreement by the Contractor or the Contractor’s Personnel shall constitute a breach of the Contract between the Contractor and the State.
perturbation.changed_text: A breach of this Agreement by the Contractor shall constitute a breach of the Contract between the Contractor and the State.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'or the Contractor’s Personnel', the contract now implies that actions of Contractor's Personnel are not direct breaches, creating a contradiction with Section 8. Section 8 holds the contractor responsible for the actions of its personnel concerning Confidential Information. If the Personnel's actions are not a breach of the NDA, can Section 8 still be enforced based on their actions? It introduces uncertainty, resulting in an in-text contradiction in Section 8 on the liability of the contractor for its personnel's actions.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Contractor acknowledges that any failur e by the Contractor or the Contractor’s Personnel to abide by the terms and conditions of use of the Confidential Information may cause irreparable harm to the State and that monetary damages may be inadequate to compensate the State for such breach. Accor dingly, the Contractor agrees that the State may obtain an injunction to prevent the disclosure, copying or improper use of the Confidential Information. The Contractor consents to personal jurisdiction in the Maryland State Courts. The State’s rights and remedies hereunder are cumulative and the State expressly reserves any and all rights, remedies, claims and actions that it may have now or in the future to protect the Confidential Information and seek damages from the Contractor and the Contractor’s Personnel for a failure to comply with the requirements of this Agreement. In the event the State suffers any losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, or costs (including, by way of example only, attorneys’ fees and disbursements) that are attributable, in whole or in part to any failure by the Contractor or any of the Contractor’s Personnel to comply with the requirements of this Agreement, the Contractor shall hold harmless and indemnify the State from and against any such losses, damages, liabilities, expenses, and costs.
perturbation.justification: The original text states "A breach of this Agreement by the Contractor or the Contractor’s Personnel shall constitute a breach..." while the changed text says "A breach of this Agreement by the Contractor shall constitute a breach...". The change removes the phrase "or the Contractor's Personnel." This means that a breach by the Contractor's Personnel is no longer explicitly a breach of the agreement. However, the contradicted text explicitly states "Contractor acknowledges that any failur e by the Contractor or the Contractor’s Personnel to abide by the terms..." and proceeds to hold the contractor responsible for its personnel. By removing the Contractor's Personnel from the clause defining breach, and failing to define what level of actions they are responsible for in other sections, it creates uncertainty as to whether their actions would result in a breach by the contractor. Therefore, the changed text creates an in-text contradiction by implying personnel breaches are not the Contractor's breaches, while the contradicted text still holds the Contractor liable for actions of the Contractor's Personnel. This aligns with the 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' definition, because essential language regarding the contractor's personnel was removed, creating a contradiction.
